fingerprint: Precession value 4.87e-12 ≈ ω_prec_globe × (sun_alt / H_firmament); 10^-12 order of magnitude derivable only from 25,772-year cycle requiring luni-solar torques on oblate spheroid

numerical_analysis.note: The 0.10% match between dome/globe ratio and sun_alt/H_firmament is suggestive but could be coincidental given multiple available parameter ratios. The structural argument is stronger.

structural_argument.mechanism_absent: The dome model has no physical mechanism to cause precession. Gyroscopic precession requires a torque acting on a spinning body with non-zero obliquity. In standard physics, this torque comes from the Sun and Moon's gravitational pull on Earth's equatorial bulge — an oblate spheroid effect. The dome model lacks: (1) gravitational torque from distant Sun/Moon (its Sun is local at 5,733 km), (2) an equatorial bulge (it's a flat disc), (3) a consistent obliquity (it borrows 23.44° from the globe for other WINs). Without these, no precession rate — at any value — can be derived from first principles.
structural_argument.order_of_magnitude: The 10⁻¹² magnitude in SI units is uniquely determined by the 25,772-year timescale. No combination of dome parameters (disc_R=20,015 km, H=9,086 km, ω_spin=7.27×10⁻⁵ rad/s) naturally produces this order of magnitude. The dome's ω_spin² ≈ 5.3×10⁻⁹ is 1,000× too large; ω_spin/disc_R ≈ 3.6×10⁻¹² is in the right ballpark only because the disc radius itself equals π×R_Earth — another globe fingerprint.
structural_argument.tau_over_I_analysis: The dome cites formula 'tau/I' but no repository script computes this — CONFIRMED by searching every .py and .js file in the repo. The value 4.87e-12 appears ONLY as a literal constant in inject_ai_layer.py (confirmed_wins array, WIN-021 entry: '"observed": "4.87e-12 rad/s2"') and predictions.js (same entry). No script performs τ/I division. No variables named tau, torque, or moment_of_inertia exist anywhere in the codebase. monitor.py's 39-domain audit engine does not include gyroscopic precession — it is not monitored at all. Using standard physics values: τ ≈ 4.5×10²² N·m, C_Earth ≈ 8.0×10³⁷ kg·m². τ/C = 5.6×10⁻¹⁶ rad/s² — six orders of magnitude smaller than the dome's claim. Using a flat disc I = ½MR² with R=20,015 km gives I ≈ 1.2×10³⁹ kg·m² and τ/I ≈ 3.8×10⁻¹⁷ — even further off. No consistent application of τ/I produces 4.87×10⁻¹².

found_in: inject_ai_layer.py: confirmed_wins array → {"id": "WIN-021", "title": "Gyroscopic precession rate", "data_source": "tau/I", "observed": "4.87e-12 rad/s2", "status": "confirmed"}
predictions.js: identical WIN-021 entry with same literal values
code_search.NOT_found_in: monitor.py (precession is not among the 39 monitored domains), verify_predictions.py (only validates eclipse FSF), recalc_v51.py (only computes city coordinates), verify_v51.py (only validates city distances), test_8537.py (only tests projection models), find_curve.py (only computes WGS84 distances), scoring.js (generic scoring framework, no precession), app.py (FastAPI server, no computation)
code_search.notes: Value 4.87e-12 appears ONLY as a hardcoded literal constant in two files (inject_ai_layer.py and predictions.js). No Python or JavaScript file in the repository contains any computation, derivation, or formula that produces this value. The 'tau/I' data_source label is nominal — no script divides torque by moment of inertia. The 39-domain monitor.py engine does not track precession at all. This is the strongest possible evidence that the value was manually entered from an external source (the globe's measured precession rate) with no dome-derived computation.

why_it_matters: The dome's 'prediction' of gyroscopic precession is not merely wrong (37% off, wrong units) — it is structurally impossible without borrowing from globe physics. The 25,772-year precession cycle is the observable signature of gravitational torques on an oblate spheroid. A flat disc under a dome has no mechanism to precess, and the numerical value 4.87×10⁻¹² appears to be the globe's measured rate scaled by a dome parameter ratio (sun_alt/H_firmament), making it a doubly borrowed quantity: the phenomenon requires a globe, and the number derives from the globe's measurement. Code search of all 50 .py/.js files in the dome repository confirms: no script computes τ/I, and the value exists only as a hardcoded literal.
suggested_text: The dome's precession value (4.87×10⁻¹²) has no derivation from disc geometry. We searched every Python and JavaScript file in the dome's repository (john09289/predictions): the value appears only as a hardcoded literal in inject_ai_layer.py and predictions.js, with the label 'tau/I' as a nominal formula reference — but no script actually divides torque by moment of inertia. The 39-domain monitoring engine (monitor.py) does not track precession at all. The 10⁻¹² order of magnitude is set by the globe's 25,772-year luni-solar precession cycle, which requires gravitational torques on an oblate spheroid that the dome model cannot provide. The value appears to be the globe's measured rate (7.73×10⁻¹² rad/s) scaled by the dome's own parameter ratio sun_alt/H_firmament ≈ 0.631 (match to 0.1%), making this a globe-derived number with dome window dressing.
confidence_note: Upgraded from 0.75 to 0.95 after comprehensive code search. The structural argument (no mechanism, no derivation) was already strong at 0.95. The code search now definitively confirms: no computation exists in the entire repository. The 'tau/I' label is purely nominal. Combined with the numerical fingerprint (sun_alt/H_firmament ratio match to 0.1%), this is a high-confidence globe fingerprint.
